headline: Last Tree of Its Kind Gets a Second Chance
meta_description: Conservationists collected seeds from the world's last wild Dendroseris neriifolia tree on Robinson Crusoe Island to prevent extinction.
summary_short: Scientists have rescued seeds from the final wild tree of an endangered species clinging to a Chilean cliff, offering hope for its survival.
body:
On Robinson Crusoe Island in the South Pacific, a single tree clings to a remote cliff—the last wild individual of Dendroseris neriifolia on Earth. At roughly 150 years old, this isolated specimen represents the final chance to save the species from extinction.

Conservationists recently made a perilous journey to collect seeds from the tree's one-seeded fruits. Of 400 seeds gathered, 24 proved viable—a modest haul that carries outsized importance. Using X-ray imaging, researchers at the Millennium Seed Bank at Kew confirmed these seeds showed embryo development, making them suitable for germination trials.

The strategy is ambitious: eight seeds will be grown for immediate testing, another eight germinated once methods are proven, and the final eight preserved long-term at Kew. Seedlings will be distributed to U.K. botanical gardens to refine cultivation techniques under various conditions.

But seeds alone won't restore the species. All 11 Dendroseris trees exist only in the Juan Fernández Archipelago off Chile, and all face similar threats—invasive plants, goats, and rodents that have devastated their habitats. These trees also sustain the critically endangered Juan Fernández firecrown hummingbird.

Conservationists acknowledge the journey ahead requires habitat restoration, invasive species control, and carefully planned reintroductions. Yet by preserving genetic diversity now, they're creating the possibility of bringing this ancient tree back from the brink.
